definition: A momentum oscillator measuring the speed and magnitude of price changes, ranging 0-100. RSI above 70 is conventionally overbought; below 30 is oversold. Useful for identifying momentum exhaustion, divergences, and entry/exit timing.
formal_definition: RSI_n = 100 - 100/(1 + RS), where RS = EMA_n(gains) / EMA_n(losses). Welles Wilder (1978) original: n=14 periods. RSI ∈ [0, 100]; >70 = overbought, <30 = oversold.
operational_definition: 14-period Wilder smoothed RSI: separate exponential moving averages of upward and downward price changes. Widely available via yfinance, TA-Lib, pandas-ta. Signals: >70 sell, <30 buy (contrarian) or directional (momentum).

definition: Risk-adjusted return metric: (portfolio return minus risk-free rate) divided by portfolio standard deviation. Measures return earned per unit of total risk. Used to compare strategies, optimize portfolios, and evaluate fund performance.
formal_definition: S = (R_p - R_f) / σ_p, where R_p is portfolio return, R_f is risk-free rate, and σ_p is the standard deviation of portfolio excess returns.
operational_definition: Annualized: (mean monthly excess return / std dev monthly excess return) × √12. Computed from time-series of portfolio returns relative to T-bill rate.

definition: The largest peak-to-trough decline in portfolio or security value over a given period. Measures tail risk and the psychological challenge of a strategy. Calmar ratio = annualized return / max drawdown.
formal_definition: MDD = min_{t∈[0,T]} [(P_t - max_{s∈[0,t]} P_s) / max_{s∈[0,t]} P_s], the largest peak-to-trough percentage decline in cumulative portfolio value over a given period.
operational_definition: Computed from daily or monthly cumulative return series: rolling maximum drawdown = max percentage loss from any prior peak to any subsequent trough.

definition: The maximum expected loss over a given time horizon at a specified confidence level. CVaR measures the expected loss beyond the VaR threshold. Standard risk management metric for position sizing and portfolio risk.
formal_definition: VaR_{α}(X) = -inf{x ∈ ℝ : P(X ≤ x) > α}, the negative of the α-quantile of the portfolio return distribution. For 1-day 99% VaR: the loss not exceeded with 99% probability.
operational_definition: Historical simulation (empirical quantile of past returns), parametric (mean - z_α × σ), or Monte Carlo simulation. Backtested by counting VaR violations over a holdout period.

definition: Beta measures a security's sensitivity to market-wide movements. CAPM decomposes total risk into systematic (beta, compensated) and idiosyncratic (diversifiable, uncompensated) components.
formal_definition: β_i = Cov(R_i, R_m) / Var(R_m), the slope coefficient from OLS regression of asset returns on market returns. β=1 means asset moves 1-for-1 with market; β>1 means amplified market exposure.
operational_definition: OLS regression of rolling 60-month excess returns on market excess return (CAPM). Also estimated via 5-factor regressions (Fama-French) for adjusted beta.

definition: The empirical tendency for securities with strong recent performance (typically 12-month return excluding the most recent month) to continue outperforming over the next 3-12 months. One of the most robust anomalies in finance — Jegadeesh and Titman (1993) documented roughly 1% per month excess returns to a long-short momentum strategy.
formal_definition: MOM_{t} = R_{t-12,t-2}, the cumulative return from 12 months ago to 1 month ago (skipping most recent month to avoid bid-ask bounce). Jegadeesh-Titman (1993) specification.
operational_definition: 12-1 month prior cumulative return ranking. Top decile = winners, bottom decile = losers. Zero-cost long-short portfolio return measures the momentum premium.

definition: The tendency for stocks with low price relative to fundamentals (low P/B, low P/E) to earn higher long-run returns than growth stocks. A core component of the Fama-French factor model. Debate continues about risk compensation vs. behavioral mispricing.
formal_definition: HML_t = (1/2)(SmallValue + BigValue) - (1/2)(SmallGrowth + BigGrowth), where Value = top 30% B/M, Growth = bottom 30% B/M, formed annually at June end using prior December B/M.
operational_definition: Book-to-market ratio (B/M): book equity from prior fiscal year / market equity at prior December. Fama-French HML factor from Ken French data library (monthly returns available 1926-present).

definition: The degree to which asset prices fully reflect all available information. Fama's weak form (prices reflect past prices), semi-strong form (prices reflect all public info), and strong form (prices reflect all info including private). A central organizing concept in finance that defines whether systematic excess returns are possible.
formal_definition: Fama (1970) taxonomy: Weak form — prices reflect all past prices; Semi-strong — all public information; Strong — all information including private. Tested via abnormal return = 0 post-event.
operational_definition: Event study methodology: abnormal return = actual return - expected return (CAPM or factor model). Joint-test problem: any rejection tests efficiency AND the pricing model simultaneously.

definition: The tendency for asset prices or returns to revert toward a long-run average after extreme moves. DeBondt and Thaler (1985) documented 3-5 year reversal in stock returns. Contrasts with short-term momentum. Basis of contrarian investing and pairs trading.
formal_definition: AR(1) spread process: dS_t = κ(μ - S_t)dt + σ dW_t, where κ is the speed of mean reversion. Half-life = ln(2)/κ. High κ → fast reversion; κ→0 → random walk.
operational_definition: OLS regression of spread changes on lagged spread level: ΔS_t = α + β·S_{t-1} + ε_t. Half-life = -ln(2)/β. Augmented Dickey-Fuller test for stationarity of spread.

definition: Statistical arbitrage strategy exploiting mean-reverting spread between two cointegrated securities. When spread diverges beyond historical norms (z-score above 2), trade converges it back. Common pairs: stocks in same sector, ETF vs. components.
formal_definition: Two price series P_A and P_B are cointegrated if ∃ β such that P_A - β·P_B ~ I(0). Spread z-score: z_t = (spread_t - μ_spread) / σ_spread, where spread_t = P_A,t - β̂·P_B,t - intercept.
operational_definition: Engle-Granger two-step: (1) OLS regression P_A on P_B to estimate hedge ratio β; (2) ADF test on residuals. Spread z-score computed from rolling 252-day mean and std. Entry at |z|>2, exit at |z|<0.5.

definition: Technical indicators derived from price averages over different windows (SMA/EMA). Common signals include price crossing MA for trend entry, MA crossovers such as the golden cross, and distance from MA for mean reversion timing.
formal_definition: MA crossover signal: buy when short MA > long MA; sell when short MA < long MA. SMA_n = (1/n)Σ_{i=0}^{n-1} P_{t-i}. EMA_n = α·P_t + (1-α)·EMA_{t-1}, α = 2/(n+1).
operational_definition: Common parameters: SMA(50) vs SMA(200) for golden/death cross; MACD = EMA(12) - EMA(26), signal = EMA(9) of MACD. Brock et al (1992) tested 1-50 and 1-150 day VMA rules.

definition: Price envelopes that expand and contract with volatility. Bollinger Bands (price plus/minus 2 standard deviations of MA) identify overextension and squeeze setups. Average True Range (ATR) measures volatility for position sizing and stop placement.
formal_definition: Bollinger Bands: Upper = SMA_n + k·σ_n; Lower = SMA_n - k·σ_n, where σ_n is rolling standard deviation over n periods. Default: n=20, k=2. %B = (Price - Lower)/(Upper - Lower).
operational_definition: %B position within bands: %B > 1 (above upper band) = overbought; %B < 0 (below lower) = oversold. ATR(14) = Wilder EMA of max(High-Low, |High-PrevClose|, |Low-PrevClose|). Both available via TA-Lib.

definition: The principle that price moves are more reliable when accompanied by above-average volume. On-Balance Volume (OBV) accumulates volume on up-days, subtracts on down-days — divergence between OBV and price signals potential reversal.
formal_definition: On-Balance Volume: OBV_t = OBV_{t-1} + sign(P_t - P_{t-1}) × V_t. Volume ratio: V_t / SMA_20(V). Price-volume divergence: price rising with falling OBV signals distribution.
operational_definition: OBV computed cumulatively from daily close prices and volume. Volume ratio = daily volume / 20-day average volume. OBV trend direction compared to price trend direction for confirmation/divergence signal.

definition: Price levels where buying or selling pressure historically concentrated, causing price to pause or reverse. Derived from prior highs/lows, moving averages, volume nodes, and round numbers. Breakouts above resistance signal potential trend continuation.
formal_definition: Support level S: price level where buying pressure historically exceeded selling pressure, preventing further decline. Resistance R: price where selling exceeded buying. Breakout = close beyond R with volume confirmation.
operational_definition: Identified via: (1) local minima/maxima on rolling n-period windows; (2) high-volume historical price clusters; (3) Fibonacci retracements (23.6%, 38.2%, 61.8% of prior range). Tested via breakout return over subsequent k-day holding period.

definition: The standard deviation of portfolio returns, driven by asset volatilities and pairwise correlations. Markowitz (1952) showed that combining low-correlation assets reduces portfolio risk below the weighted average of individual asset risks.
formal_definition: σ_p = √(w'Σw), where w is the vector of portfolio weights and Σ is the N×N covariance matrix of asset returns. Annualized: σ_p,annual = σ_p,monthly × √12.
operational_definition: Sample covariance matrix from T-period return history; annualized by multiplying by frequency (252 for daily, 12 for monthly). Shrinkage estimators (Ledoit-Wolf) used for large N. Risk-parity weighting sets w_i ∝ 1/σ_i.

definition: The market's forward-looking estimate of a security's volatility, extracted from options prices via Black-Scholes. IV rank compares current IV to its 52-week range. High IV rank favors premium-selling strategies; low IV rank favors buying options. VIX is the implied volatility of S&P 500 options.
formal_definition: σ_IV = BS^{-1}(C_market; S, K, T, r), the volatility parameter that equates the Black-Scholes model price to the observed market option price. VIX = √(E^Q[∫_0^T σ²_t dt] × (1/T)) × 100.
operational_definition: Computed by numerical inversion (Newton-Raphson) of Black-Scholes formula for each option. VIX uses model-free approach: VIX² = (2/T)Σ_i (ΔK_i/K_i²) × e^{rT} × Q(K_i) - (F/K_0 - 1)². IV rank = (current IV - 52w low)/(52w high - 52w low) × 100.

definition: Sensitivity measures for options prices: Delta (price sensitivity to underlying), Gamma (delta sensitivity), Theta (time decay), Vega (sensitivity to IV changes), Rho (sensitivity to interest rates). Essential for understanding options P&L and managing positions.
formal_definition: Δ = ∂C/∂S; Γ = ∂²C/∂S²; Θ = ∂C/∂t; Ν = ∂C/∂σ; ρ = ∂C/∂r. For European call (Black-Scholes): Δ = N(d₁), Γ = n(d₁)/(S·σ·√T), Ν = S·n(d₁)·√T.
operational_definition: Greeks derived analytically from Black-Scholes formula or computed numerically via finite-difference for American/exotic options. Delta-hedging: trade -Δ shares per option to neutralize directional exposure. Available from yfinance options chain + py_vollib.

definition: The ratio of put option volume to call option volume. High ratio signals bearish sentiment; low ratio signals bullish sentiment. Used as contrarian indicator — extreme readings can precede reversals. Equity P/C ratio below 0.5 or above 1.0 are notable extremes.
formal_definition: PCR = Total put volume / Total call volume. Index PCR uses S&P 500 options (CBOE). Equity PCR uses single-stock options. PCR > 1.0 signals elevated bearish positioning; PCR < 0.7 signals bullish complacency.
operational_definition: Daily CBOE equity and index put/call volume ratios published by CBOE. Smoothed via 10-day or 21-day moving average to reduce noise. Sentiment signal: PCR > 1.2 = extreme fear (contrarian buy), PCR < 0.6 = extreme greed (contrarian sell).

definition: Aggregate investor optimism or pessimism beyond what fundamentals justify. High sentiment inflates speculative stocks; low sentiment depresses them. Measured by Baker-Wurgler index, put/call ratios, fund flows, or survey measures.
formal_definition: Baker-Wurgler (2006) sentiment index: first principal component of 6 proxies (closed-end fund discount, NYSE turnover, IPO volume, first-day IPO returns, seasoned equity share, dividend premium), each orthogonalized to macro variables.
operational_definition: PCA composite of: (1) closed-end fund discount, (2) NYSE share turnover, (3) IPO number, (4) IPO average first-day return, (5) new equity share in total issuance, (6) dividend premium. Orthogonalized to GDP growth, consumption, industrial production, unemployment, inflation.

definition: A security's loading on systematic risk factors that explain cross-sectional return variation. Fama-French 3-factor model includes market, size (SMB), and value (HML). Five-factor model adds profitability (RMW) and investment (CMA). Factor alpha is return unexplained by factor exposures.
formal_definition: Factor loading β_k: from time-series OLS R_i,t = α_i + Σ_k β_{i,k}·F_{k,t} + ε_{i,t}. Fama-French 5-factor: F = {MKT-RF, SMB, HML, RMW, CMA}. Alpha (Jensen's α) = annualized intercept.
operational_definition: OLS regression of monthly excess returns on Ken French factor returns (available at mba.tuck.dartmouth.edu/pages/faculty/ken.french/data_library.html). Rolling 36-month or 60-month estimation window. t-stat on alpha from Newey-West standard errors.

definition: Post-Earnings Announcement Drift (PEAD) — the tendency for stock prices to continue drifting in the direction of an earnings surprise for weeks after announcement. One of the strongest violations of semi-strong EMH.
formal_definition: SUE (Standardized Unexpected Earnings) = (EPS_actual - EPS_expected) / σ_forecast_error. Scaled by standard deviation of prior forecast errors to normalize across firms and periods.
operational_definition: Measured via: (1) I/B/E/S analyst consensus vs. actual EPS; (2) seasonal random walk: EPS_t - EPS_{t-4} scaled by price. Extreme decile sorts (SUE 1 vs 10) form long-short PEAD portfolio. 60-day drift window standard per Bernard & Thomas (1989).

definition: The ease with which a security can be bought or sold without causing significant price impact. Measured by bid-ask spread, market depth, and trading volume. Low liquidity amplifies volatility and increases trading costs.
formal_definition: Amihud (2002) illiquidity ratio: ILLIQ_{i,t} = (1/T)Σ_{d=1}^{T} |R_{i,d}| / DVOL_{i,d}, where R is daily return and DVOL is daily dollar volume. Higher ILLIQ = less liquid.
operational_definition: Bid-ask spread: (ask - bid)/midpoint × 100. Amihud ratio from daily return and volume data. Turnover = shares traded / shares outstanding. Pastor-Stambaugh (2002) liquidity factor measures return reversal sensitivity to volume-signed order flow.

definition: The study of trading mechanisms, order types, market makers, and how market design affects price formation and trading costs. Covers informed vs. uninformed trading, adverse selection, and the economics of market making.
formal_definition: Kyle's λ: price impact parameter where ΔP_t = λ·x_t + ε_t, and x_t is order flow (signed volume). Market depth = 1/(2λ). Roll (1984) effective spread = 2√(-Cov(ΔP_t, ΔP_{t-1})).
operational_definition: Estimated from intraday data: regress mid-quote return on signed trade volume to get price impact. Effective spread from CRSP daily data using Roll's serial covariance estimator. TAQ data for tick-by-tick microstructure analysis.

definition: The process by which markets aggregate dispersed information from buyers and sellers into a single observed price. Driven by informed trading, order flow, and arbitrage. Quality of price discovery affects how quickly prices reflect fundamental value.
formal_definition: Information Share (Hasbrouck 1995): IS_i = (e_i·F)²/Var(m_t), where e_i is the ith cointegrating vector coefficient and F is the Cholesky factor of the innovation covariance matrix.
operational_definition: Vector Error Correction Model (VECM) estimated on synchronized price series across venues (stock, futures, options). Information share computed from Hasbrouck (1995) or Gonzalo-Granger (1995) decomposition. Available for spot vs. futures, NYSE vs. dark pools.

definition: The practice of shifting capital between market sectors based on relative momentum, economic cycle positioning, or valuation. Tracked via sector ETFs. Strong relative momentum sectors are overweighted; weak sectors are underweighted.
formal_definition: Sector momentum return = Σ_{j∈W} R_{j,t} / |W| - Σ_{j∈L} R_{j,t} / |L|, where W = top-K sector ETFs by prior 12-1 month return, L = bottom-K. Moskowitz-Grinblatt (1999): K=1 sector.
operational_definition: Sector ETF returns: SPDRs (XLK, XLV, XLF, XLE, XLI, XLP, XLU, XLRE, XLY, XLB, XLC). Formation period: prior 12 months (skip last month). Holding period: 1 month. Rebalanced monthly. Benchmark: equal-weighted sector average.

definition: The excess expected return of equity investments over a risk-free benchmark; central premium in factor-pricing models.
The historical average excess return of small-capitalization stocks over large-capitalization stocks; the SMB factor in Fama-French models.
